communicative_purpose: Expressing a state of melancholy or depression
trigger_situations: When feeling sad, down, or depressed; after bad news; during difficult times
contexts: Personal conversations about feelings, describing mood, informal settings
variations: avoir le cafard, être cafard, avoir une depression (more formal)
common_mistakes: Using 'être cafard' instead of 'avoir le cafard'; confusing with literal cockroach
collocates: avoir le cafard, très cafard, le cafard m'envahit
example_2: 'avoir le cafard' est une expression utile.

communicative_purpose: Expressing fear or nervousness about something
trigger_situations: Before an exam, when facing something intimidating, during frightening situations
contexts: Informal conversations, expressing personal fears, casual settings
variations: avoir les jetons, avoir la frousse (similar), avoir peur (standard)
common_mistakes: Confusing with other fear expressions; using avoir instead of être for permanent fear states
collocates: avoir les jetons, j'ai les jetons, tu me donnes les jetons
example_2: 'avoir les jetons' est une expression utile.
example_2_translation: 'have the chips' is a useful expression.
example_3_translation: I know how to have the chips.

meaning: to sulk/to have a long face

trigger_situations: After an argument, when things don't go one's way, when sulking

meaning: to have a heavy heart/to be very sad
communicative_purpose: Expressing deep sadness or grief, often from a difficult situation
trigger_situations: During mourning, after a breakup, when facing hardship, during farewells
contexts: Serious personal conversations, expressing condolences, emotional discussions

collocates: avoir le cœur gros, le cœur gros, avoir le cœur gros de

example_3: Je connais avoir le cœur gros.

meaning: to feel worthless/to feel like nothing
communicative_purpose: Expressing a loss of self-esteem or feeling of worthlessness
trigger_situations: After failure, during depression, when comparing oneself unfavorably to others
contexts: Personal reflections, therapy contexts, informal emotional conversations
variations: se sentir zéro, se sentir nul, ne rien valer (similar)
common_mistakes: Using 'être zéro' instead of 'se sentir zéro'; literal interpretation
collocates: se sentir zéro, je me sens zéro, tu te sens zéro

chunk_text: avoir des papillons dans le ventre
meaning: to have butterflies in one's stomach
communicative_purpose: Expressing nervousness or excitement, often positive anticipation
trigger_situations: Before a date, before a presentation, when excited about something
contexts: Romantic contexts, professional situations, casual conversations about feelings
variations: avoir des papillons dans le ventre, avoir le trac (similar but more stage fright)
common_mistakes: Confusing the preposition 'dans' with others; using singular form incorrectly
patterns: avoir + des + papillons + dans + le + ventre
collocates: avoir des papillons, j'ai des papillons, des papillons dans le ventre
example_1: avoir + des + papillons + dans + le + ventre
example_2: 'avoir des papillons dans le ventre' est une expression utile.
example_3: Je connais avoir des papillons dans le ventre.
example_1_translation: avoir + des + papillons + dans + le + ventre

example_3_translation: I know I have butterflies in my stomach.

meaning: to have a meltdown/to go crazy

meaning: to be down in the dumps/to have zero morale

meaning: to burst into tears/to melt into tears

meaning: to have a tight throat/to feel choked up
communicative_purpose: Expressing emotion that prevents speaking, often sadness or fear
trigger_situations: During emotional conversations, when holding back tears, from fear
contexts: Serious personal conversations, describing emotional reactions, formal settings

meaning: to cry bitterly/to cry one's heart out
communicative_purpose: Describing intense, uncontrollable crying, often from nostalgia or grief
trigger_situations: During mourning, when remembering past trauma, when overwhelmed by emotion
contexts: Personal stories, describing emotional breakdowns, literary contexts
variations: pleurer comme une madeleine, pleurer à chaudes larmes (similar), pleurer toutes les larmes de son corps

meaning: to have the blues/to feel melancholy
communicative_purpose: Expressing a general feeling of sadness or dissatisfaction without specific cause
trigger_situations: During gray weather, without apparent reason, during difficult periods
contexts: Casual conversations about mood, describing general melancholy, informal contexts

meaning: to be at bay/to be desperate
communicative_purpose: Describing a situation of extreme desperation, with no way out

meaning: to laugh bitterly/to force a laugh
communicative_purpose: Describing insincere laughter that masks discomfort or bitter feelings
trigger_situations: During awkward situations, when someone laughs off something painful

communicative_purpose: Expressing physical nausea or figuratively, strong disgust
trigger_situations: During illness, when seeing something disgusting, when facing something repulsive
contexts: Medical contexts, describing reactions to unpleasant things, physical descriptions
